meta.question: Does violent video game exposure increase aggression?

factors.label: Researcher-expectancy effects
factors.rationale: The authors argue that methodological bias from expectancy effects inflates reported effects, a point contested by critics.

provenance.quote: However, effect sizes appeared to be elevated because of non-best-practices and researcher-expectancy effects, particularly for experimental studies.

Anderson et al. included many studies that do not relate well to serious aggression, an apparently biased sample of unpublished studies, and a "best practices" analysis that appears unreliable and does not consider the impact of unstandardized aggression measures on the inflation of effect size estimates.

Rather, indulgence in such claims risked damage to the credibility of games effects research, credibility which can only be restored through better empirical research and more conservative and careful statements by scholars.

Third, experiments meeting the original authors' criteria for methodological quality do not yield larger adjusted effects than other experiments, but instead yield larger indications of bias, indicating that perhaps they were selected for significance.

Ultimately, even in light of potential methodological problems, suboptimal reproducibility, and researcher degrees of freedom, as noted by de Vrieze (2018) , we believe that these conflicting meta-analyses in fact provide considerable consensus in favor of consistent, but small, detrimental effects of violent video games on aggressive behavior.

factors.label: Effect size magnitude
factors.rationale: The practical importance of the observed increase is debated across camps.

provenance.quote: Despite seemingly conflicting results for the statistical significance of the pooled estimates in different meta-analyses of video-game studies, all of the meta-analyses do in fact point to the conclusion that, in the vast majority of settings, violent video games do increase aggressive behavior but that these effects are almost always quite small.
However, upon closer examination, the effect sizes reported in Ferguson’s 2015 meta-analysis are non-zero and were interpreted by the author as “minimal” but not null effects.

The current analysis revealed negligible relationships between violent games and aggressive or prosocial behavior, small relationships with aggressive affect and cognitions, and stronger relationships with desensitization.

refused.title: Violent video games and real-world violence: Rhetoric versus data.
refused.reason: The study tests violent-crime trends, not the case question's aggression outcome; its text concedes a possible link with minor aggression.

refused.title: Longitudinal effects of media violence on aggression and empathy among German adolescents
refused.reason: Combined film, television, and game exposure cannot isolate the video-game exposure asked by this case.

refused.title: The public health risks of media violence: a meta-analytic review.
refused.reason: Mixed-media meta-analysis does not provide a game-specific estimate for this case.

refused.title: Selling violent video game solutions: A look inside the APA's internal notes leading to the creation of the APA's 2005 resolution on violence in video games and interactive media
refused.reason: Institutional-policy history and court treatment, not evidence of a causal aggression effect.

refused.title: Possible Biases of Researchers’ Attitudes Toward Video Games: Publication Trends Analysis of the Medical Literature (1980–2013)
refused.reason: Bibliometric researcher-attitudes study with no aggression outcome; belongs in methodological context rather than an effect position.

sources.title: Nailing the Coffin Shut on Doubts that Violent Video Games Stimulate Aggression ∼Comment on Anderson et al. (2010)

position.quote: The current meta-analysis by Anderson and his colleagues is the best yet in proving beyond a reasonable doubt that exposure to video game violence increases the risk that the observer will behave more aggressively and violently in the future.

sources.title: Technical Report on the Review of the Violent Video Game Literature

provenance.quote: All four meta-analyses reported an adverse effect of violent video game use on aggressive outcomes, with an effect size greater than zero and a narrow range of unadjusted effect sizes (.14–.29).

position.quote: All four meta-analyses reported an adverse effect of violent video game use on aggressive outcomes, with an effect size greater than zero and a narrow range of unadjusted effect sizes (.14–.29).

sources.title: Violent video game engagement is not associated with adolescents' aggressive behaviour: evidence from a registered report

provenance.quote: = 1004) aged 14 and 15 years and an equal number of their carers were interviewed.

position.quote: Results did not support this prediction, nor did they support the idea that the relationship between these factors follows a nonlinear parabolic function.

fundingDetails: John Fell Fund, University of Oxford

sources.title: The Question of Violent Video Games and Aggression: Testing Statistical and Methodological Issues of Null Effects Using Data From an Open‐Access Case Study

provenance.quote: One recent failure to replicate established violent video game effects (Przybylski and Weinstein 2019) has made its data set publicly available, thereby offering a unique opportunity to examine the hypothesis that methodological and statistical problems underlie some replication failures.

position.quote: Overall, results demonstrate that conceptual misunderstandings of aggression, poor measures regarding both aggression and video game violence exposure, and inappropriate statistical procedures contributed to the initially reported null results.

sources.title: Exposure to violent video games and aggression in German adolescents: a longitudinal analysis

provenance.quote: A total of 295 German adolescents completed the measures of violent video game usage, endorsement of aggressive norms, hostile attribution bias, and physical as well as indirect/relational aggression cross-sectionally, and a subsample of N=143 was measured again 30 months later.

position.quote: Longitudinal analyses using path analysis showed that violence exposure at T1 predicted physical (but not indirect/relational) aggression 30 months later, whereas aggression at T1 was unrelated to later video game use.

sources.title: A longitudinal study of the association between violent video game play and aggression among adolescents.

provenance.quote: Adolescents (N = 1,492, 50.8% female) were surveyed annually from Grade 9 to Grade 12 about their video game play and aggressive behaviors.

position.quote: Sustained violent video game play was significantly related to steeper increases in adolescents' trajectory of aggressive behavior over time.

fundingDetails: Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council of Canada

sources.title: Do longitudinal studies support long-term relationships between aggressive game play and youth aggressive behaviour? A meta-analytic examination

position.quote: Overall, longitudinal studies do not appear to support substantive long-term links between aggressive game content and youth aggression.

sources.title: Does playing violent video games cause aggression? A longitudinal intervention study

provenance.quote: Our participants played the violent video game Grand Theft Auto V, the non-violent video game The Sims 3 or no game at all for 2 months on a daily basis.

position.quote: Since at least ten significant effects would be expected purely by chance, we conclude that there were no detrimental effects of violent video gameplay.

sources.title: The contagious impact of playing violent video games on aggression: Longitudinal evidence - PMC

provenance.quote: At Time 1, there were 2,502 participants (1,376 females, 1,126 males; mean age = 35.7 years, SD = 11.8).

position.quote: Mediation analyses showed that friends’ aggression at Time 1 accounted for the impact of friends’ amount of violent video game play at Time 1 on the participant's aggression at Time 2.

sources.title: The social contagious effects of violent video game play: retaliatory, displaced, and the mere observation of aggression

provenance.quote: The present experimental study aims to test whether exposure to violent video games increases aggression levels in players and whether this increased aggression spreads among their social network.

position.quote: Results showed that aggression after video game play is indeed contagious and that not only victims of the player’s aggression retaliate or are aggressive toward a neutral person (i.e., displaced aggression), but that even mere observers of the player’s aggression are aggressive toward a neutral person.

sources.title: Reexamining the Findings of the American Psychological Association’s 2015 Task Force on Violent Media: A Meta-Analysis

position.quote: The current analysis revealed negligible relationships between violent games and aggressive or prosocial behavior, small relationships with aggressive affect and cognitions, and stronger relationships with desensitization.

sources.title: Video Games Do Affect Social Outcomes

position.quote: Whereas violent video games increase aggression and aggression-related variables and decrease prosocial outcomes, prosocial video games have the opposite effects.

sources.title: Apples, Oranges, and the Burden of Proof – Putting Media Violence Findings Into Context

provenance.quote: In this comment on Elson and Ferguson (2013) , areas of agreement are noted in terms of the need to thoroughly and scientifically document the boundary conditions under which violent video games most impact players.

position.quote: However it is argued, in contrast to Elson and Ferguson, that violent media generally (and violent video games specifically) can and do increase the likelihood of aggressive behavior and desensitization to violence, and are linked to increases in aggressive attitudes and beliefs.

sources.title: Pay No Attention to That Data Behind the Curtain

provenance.quote: This article responds to five comments on my "Angry Birds" meta-analysis of video game influences on children (Ferguson, 2015, this issue).

position.quote: Further evidence is also provided to support the contention that publication bias remains problematic in video game literature.

sources.title: Video Games Do Indeed Influence Children and Adolescents’ Aggression, Prosocial Behavior, and Academic Performance

position.quote: Despite a variety of methodological weaknesses in his meta-analysis, Ferguson (2015, this issue) presents evidence to support the positive association between violent media consumption and a number of poor developmental outcomes.

sources.title: The effects of violent video games on aggression..

provenance.quote: This study cumulates findings across existing empirical research on the effects of violent video games to estimate overall effect size and discern important trends and moderating variables.

position.quote: Results suggest there is a smaller effect of violent video games on aggression than has been found with television violence on aggression.

sources.title: Violent Video Games and Hostile Expectations: A Test of the General Aggression Model

position.quote: People who played a violent video game described the main character as behaving more aggressively, thinking more aggressive thoughts, and feeling more angry than did people who played a nonviolent video game.

sources.title: Violent Video Games and Aggression

position.quote: Although males were more aggressive than females, neither randomized exposure to violent-video-game conditions nor previous real-life exposure to violent video games caused any differences in aggression.

sources.title: Effects of Violent Video Games on Aggressive Behavior, Aggressive Cognition, Aggressive Affect, Physiological Arousal, and Prosocial Behavior: A Meta-Analytic Review of the Scientific Literature

provenance.quote: Analyses also reveal that exposure to violent video games increases physiological arousal and aggression-related thoughts and feelings.

position.quote: A metaanalytic review of the video-game research literature reveals that violent video games increase aggressive behavior in children and young adults.

sources.title: Video games and aggressive thoughts, feelings, and behavior in the laboratory and in life.
sources.venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology

provenance.quote: In Study 2, laboratory exposure to a graphically violent video game increased aggressive thoughts and behavior.

position.quote: Study 1 found that real-life violent video game play was positively related to aggressive behavior and delinquency.

sources.title: Violent video game effects on aggression, empathy, and prosocial behavior in Eastern and Western countries: A meta-analytic review.

provenance.quote: Meta-analytic procedures were used to test the effects of violent video games on aggressive behavior, aggressive cognition, aggressive affect, physiological arousal, empathy/desensitization, and prosocial behavior.

position.quote: The evidence strongly suggests that exposure to violent video games is a causal risk factor for increased aggressive behavior, aggressive cognition, and aggressive affect and for decreased empathy and prosocial behavior.

sources.title: Much ado about nothing: The misestimation and overinterpretation of violent video game effects in Eastern and Western nations: Comment on Anderson et al. (2010).

position.quote: Despite a number of methodological flaws that all appear likely to inflate effect size estimates, the final estimate of r = .15 is still indicative of only weak effects.

sources.title: Do Angry Birds Make for Angry Children? A Meta-Analysis of Video Game Influences on Children’s and Adolescents’ Aggression, Mental Health, Prosocial Behavior, and Academic Performance

provenance.quote: In the current analysis, I sought to address this gap by focusing on studies of video game influences on child and adolescent samples.

position.quote: Overall, results from 101 studies suggest that video game influences on increased aggression (r = .06), reduced prosocial behavior (r = .04), reduced academic performance (r = -.01), depressive symptoms (r = .04), and attention deficit symptoms (r = .03) are minimal.

sources.title: Angry Birds, Angry Children, and Angry Meta-Analysts

provenance.quote: In response to the concerns raised by researchers about the appropriateness of the meta-analytical methods used by Ferguson; we decided to reanalyze the data and discuss two major misconceptions about meta-analysis.

position.quote: There was a significant yet very small effect on aggressive behavior of exposure to both general, rp = 0.062, 95% CI [0.012, 0.112], and violent, rp = 0.055, 95% CI [0.019, 0.091], video games.

sources.title: Finding the Middle Ground in Violent Video Game Research

position.quote: Ferguson's comprehensive meta-analysis provides convincing data that violent video games have almost no effect on children's aggression.

sources.title: Twenty-Five Years of Research on Violence in Digital Games and Aggression

position.quote: Empirical evidence regarding the impact of violent digital games on player aggression is, at best, mixed and cannot support unambiguous claims that such games are harmful or represent a public health crisis.

sources.title: Overstated evidence for short-term effects of violent games on affect and behavior: A reanalysis of Anderson et al. (2010).

provenance.quote: In the present manuscript, we reexamine their meta-analysis using a wider array of techniques for detecting bias and adjusting effect sizes.

position.quote: Second, after adjustment for bias, the effects of violent games on aggressive behavior in experimental research are estimated as being very small, and estimates of effects on aggressive affect are much reduced.

sources.title: Violent video game effects remain a societal concern: Reply to Hilgard, Engelhardt, and Rouder (2017).

provenance.quote: Because publication bias is a potential a problem in any scientific domain, we used a comprehensive sensitivity analysis battery to examine the influence of publication bias and outliers on the experimental effects reported by Anderson et al.

position.quote: We therefore conclude that violent video game effects should remain a societal concern.

sources.title: Metaanalysis of the relationship between violent video game play and physical aggression over time
sources.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

position.quote: Conclusion On the basis of this metaanalysis, we conclude that playing violent video games is associated with greater levels of overt physical aggression over time, after accounting for prior aggression.

sources.title: A Meta-Analysis on the Longitudinal, Age-Dependent Effects of Violent Video Games on Aggression

position.quote: Consequently, we confirm the results of other meta-analyses, with the added finding of a peak of effect sizes in early adolescence.

sources.title: Finding Common Ground in Meta-Analysis “Wars” on Violent Video Games - PMC

position.quote: Despite seemingly conflicting results for the statistical significance of the pooled estimates in different meta-analyses of video-game studies, all of the meta-analyses do in fact point to the conclusion that, in the vast majority of settings, violent video games do increase aggressive behavior but that these effects are almost always quite small.

sources.title: Evidence for publication bias in video game violence effects literature: A meta-analytic review

provenance.quote: Results indicated that publication bias does exist for experimental studies of aggressive behavior, as well as for non-experimental studies of aggressive behavior and aggressive thoughts.

position.quote: Publication bias issues emerge for both experimental and non-experimental studies of aggressive behaviors.

sources.title: Research on the Effects of Violent Video Games: A Critical Analysis

position.quote: It is concluded that the extreme focus on general aggression effects is not supported by the current literature, and that social science's focus on general aggression effects distracts from research on specific at‐risk populations, and potential positive uses of some violent video games.

sources.title: APA Task Force Report on Violent Video Games

position.quote: The Task Force’s general conclusion is that the bulk of recent evidence converges with the 2015 Task Force: There is a small and reliable relationship between violent video game exposure and aggressive outcomes.

sources.title: Lacko, Machackova & Smahel — Does violence in video games impact aggression and empathy?

provenance.quote: Drawing on representative sample of 3010 Czech adolescents (aged 13–17), data were collected over four waves to measure physical and verbal aggression, and cognitive and affective empathy.

position.quote: All desensitization effects were statistically insignificant.
